case_meta.name: scotus-glossip-v-oklahoma
case_meta.description:
Supreme Court of the United States oral argument, Glossip v. Oklahoma
(docket 22-7466), argued 2024-10-09. Death penalty case from Oklahoma —
whether prosecutorial suppression of impeachment evidence requires reversal
of a capital conviction.

This is the canonical "real legal multi-speaker" benchmark for DepoDash:
domain-perfect (legal/courtroom), high-quality studio audio, official
speaker-labeled and time-aligned transcript via Oyez. Eleven speakers in
the room (8 justices + 3 attorneys); naturally polite turn-taking with
occasional brief overlaps — a realistic but not adversarial diarization
challenge.
case_meta.license: Public domain (US government work). Audio + transcript redistributable.

ground_truth.notes:
Oyez's transcript is the de-facto canonical version (the official
SCOTUS PDF transcript is identical in content but harder to parse).
Speaker turns in reference.rttm correspond exactly to Oyez's `turns`
array; text_blocks within a turn are concatenated for the WER reference.

result.transcript: We will hear argument this morning in case 22-74-66, Glossop v. Oklahoma. Mr. Waxman. Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court, Richard Glossop was convicted on the word of one man, Justin Sneed, the undisputed murderer in this case. Oklahoma has now disclosed evidence revealing that Mr. Sneed lied to the jury about his history of psychiatric treatment, including the fact that a prison psychiatrist prescribed lithium to treat his previously undiagnosed bipolar disorder. The prosecution suppressed that evidence and then failed to correct Mr. Sneed's perjured denial, just as it suppressed evidence that in the middle of trial, in violation of the Court's sequestration order, Sneed altered his testimony about the knife wounds on the victim at the urgent request of the prosecution. Mr. Waxman. Mr. Waxman. Mr. Waxman. Mr. Waxman. Mr. Waxman, you placed quite a bit of weight on the notes from Smotherman and Ackley, and from your opening statement, you clearly do not agree with them. Did you at any point get a statement from either one of the prosecutors? Yes. Did you interview them? Yes. Well, to be clear, we did get a sworn statement, which I believe is at page 960 of the joint appendix from Gary Ackley, in which, among other things, he never mentions the fact that, he never mentions the account that he has now provided to the, in the amicus brief for the Van Tries family. And as to Ms. Smotherman, Ms. Smotherman was interviewed, both by the independent, the legislature's independent counsel, and by the Attorney General's independent counsel, Mr. Duncan. She gave different answers each time, none of which was the account she's now provided in an unsworn letter attached to the Van Tries brief. Well, it would seem that because not only, you know, their reputations are being impugned, but they are central to this case. It would seem that they, an interview of these two prosecutors would be central. They suggest that their interviews were generally about prosecuting capital cases and not specifically about the details of this. Look, their current unsworn statement, appended at the very last minute for the very first time in a merits amicus brief before this Court, deserves all the benefit of the doubt that they, you know, to which they're entitled. When, in the context of Mr. Ackley did file an affidavit, it is in the record in this case, and it is not in any way consistent with his current account. Did they make themselves unavailable? Sorry to interrupt. Well, he made himself available. He provided us a declaration. No, it's, what you're saying is, would make sense if for some reason they had made themselves unavailable. They suggest that they were not, not sought out and given an opportunity.
